title: TBM 378: How To Effectively Pitch Constraints
questions: Why do teams and leaders resist WIP limits and other constraints even when they seem logically beneficial?
How can you pitch constraints so they feel empowering rather than arbitrary or restrictive?
What framing works best for different decision-making styles (concrete vs abstract, autonomy-driven vs maximizing)?
How do loss aversion and optimism bias affect acceptance of constraints?
summary: The post explains why leaders and teams resist operational constraints like WIP limits and prioritization forcing functions, arguing that the root causes are often psychological (loss aversion, optimism bias) and social (threats to autonomy/competence) rather than technical. It then offers practical ways to pitch constraints to different audiences, using evidence, heuristic framing, participation, and time-boxed experiments to make constraints feel empirical and empowering.

title: TBM 377: Time Allocation ≠ Capacity Allocation
questions: How do we measure capacity without reducing it to time tracking?
What’s the difference between design capacity, effective capacity, and time allocation?
Why do time allocation spreadsheets fail to explain real productivity and outcomes?
How should software teams account for interruptions, rework, dependencies, and waiting?
summary: The post argues that “capacity allocation” is frequently misunderstood when organizations equate it with time tracking and quarterly salary/hour reporting. It reframes capacity as an effective, system-level ability to deliver outcomes shaped by context, constraints, and long-term investments—drawing on lean and the theory of constraints. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who need more honest metrics for productivity, planning, and decision-making in product and software teams.

title: TBM 370: Dependencies Aren't Your Problem
questions: Why do dependency management exercises fail to improve delivery outcomes?
How can teams quantify the economic cost of dependencies (displaced priorities and value throughput)?
What should replace dependency tracking—value throughput, capacity allocation, or something else?
How do you create shared priorities so teams stop overcommitting and escalating less?
summary: The post argues that dependency problems are often symptoms of incentive and priority failures rather than coordination mechanics. It’s aimed at leaders and engineering managers dealing with overloaded platform teams and ineffective dependency-tracking processes, and it proposes reframing the goal from managing dependencies to maximizing value throughput and making economically sensible capacity allocation and investment decisions.

title: TBM 360: "Wait! I Thought the Team Was Overloaded?!"
questions: Why do teams say they’re overloaded, but managers insist nothing can be cut?
How can leaders tell whether “focus” is a trick question created by incentives?
What should you do when there’s no clear winner but you still must narrow bets?
How do accumulated work debt and constraints block prioritization even when the priority list looks reasonable?
search_terms: team overloaded but nothing can be cut
how to make pause/cut feel safe
summary: This post explores why front-line teams report being overloaded while middle and upper-middle managers respond that nothing can be dropped. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who need to diagnose prioritization breakdowns, especially when incentives, “no clear winner” dynamics, and accumulated work debt prevent real focus. The guidance emphasizes trusting front-line signals and making hard choices explicit rather than treating prioritization as a vague or risky exercise.

title: TBM 356: Product Party Modes
questions: What determines the right mix of work modes at any given point in time?
What is your team biased towards, and how does that bias affect overwhelm?
How do you design for mental breaks when the team is stuck in continuous high-intensity modes?
When do allocation percentages lie, and why do they misrepresent real capacity usage?
search_terms: WIP limits and human work patterns
deep work vs interruption work mode
complementary work mix for product teams
summary: This post argues that product team capacity cannot be modeled like compute or a widget factory because different kinds of work drain attention and throughput in different ways. It introduces a “party modes” framing (e.g., questing, chipping, spellcasting, firewatch, trailblazing) to help product leaders think about the right mix of effort shapes, collaboration, and slack. It’s for product managers and product ops leaders trying to reduce overwhelm and improve allocation decisions.

title: TBM 341: 10 Tips for Turning Around a Platform Team
questions: How do we turn around a platform team that keeps losing ground to ad hoc partner requests?
What prioritization and capacity controls actually work when the platform roadmap competes with constant interrupts?
How can we migrate partner requests toward self-service while the platform is still immature?
How do we get alignment and reduce overcommitment when platform and partner teams report to different org leaders?
search_terms: cross-team alignment meeting platform partner managers
summary: This post offers practical, execution-focused guidance for leaders and members of platform teams that are “underwater” due to constant external requests, misaligned incentives, and unclear prioritization. It emphasizes behavior change through enabling constraints, scope/WIP discipline, and transparent queue metrics, while also improving stakeholder alignment and accountability. Intended for platform teams and adjacent engineering/product leaders who need a structured way to regain momentum without relying on freezes or big-bang re-plans.

title: TBM 335: "Simple" Dependency Coordination
questions: How can we coordinate dependencies across teams without premature scope convergence and constant re-planning?
What focus allocation model prevents excessive context switching when teams run in fixed cycles?
How should Team Topologies interaction modes map to primary, secondary, and ad hoc work lanes?
Is it too conservative to reserve slack and cap cognitive bandwidth for ad hoc work?
search_terms: context switching cost in software teams
three focus lanes primary secondary ad hoc
heuristics for mapping interaction modes to capacity
cost of delay for cross-team work
summary: The post argues for a dependency coordination approach that replaces spreadsheet-and-estimate juggling with a focus-lane capacity model and collaboration-driven heuristics. It’s aimed at engineering leaders and delivery teams who struggle with context switching, premature scope convergence, and cross-team dependency thrash. The guidance ties Team Topologies interaction modes to how teams should allocate primary, secondary, and ad hoc work during fixed planning cycles.

title: TBM 334: The Capacity Allocation Illusion
questions: Why does time tracking and allocating team capacity to initiatives fail to reflect true software capacity?
What are the real components of software capacity beyond engineer-hours?
How can technical debt, context switching, and bureaucracy reduce effective capacity even when hours are available?
What should executives ask instead of 'where did the money go' to understand capacity and future growth?
search_terms: time allocation vs investment in capacity
summary: This post argues that in software development, treating capacity as a simple function of engineer-hours and time allocation is an illusion that hides the real system constraints and investments shaping throughput. It draws parallels to manufacturing capacity—where bottlenecks, long-term investments, and scenario uncertainty matter—to explain why leaders should focus on how teams build and sustain capacity (people, codebase, structure, culture, and friction) rather than only where salaries or hours were spent. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders trying to measure and manage capacity without falling into reductive reporting practices.

title: TBM 322: Work Shape Mix
questions: When does team independence stop being a benefit and start blocking progress?
How can leaders support teams during a transition without endorsing the status quo?
When should a company tolerate some friction rather than formalize new processes?
How can you tell normal growing pains from systemic issues that will entrench?
search_terms: independent product work internal P&L cost centers
organizational drift into process heavy transformation
summary: This post discusses how to think about an organization’s “work shape” portfolio—how teams do large complex projects, independent product work, and zero-to-one innovation—and why the mix changes as companies scale. It is aimed at product and engineering leaders who need a practical way to recognize when growing pains are becoming systemic, and when to formalize processes versus letting friction play out. It also frames a key leadership challenge: supporting teams through transitions while maintaining realism about the current state.

title: TBM 314: Using Enabling Constraints for Situational Awareness
questions: How can we catch organizational incoherence before it becomes irreversible during rapid growth?
What enabling constraints (guardrails, gates, WIP limits) create situational awareness without killing autonomy?
What risks and constraints matter when the organization is optimizing for efficiency instead of growth?
How should teams prioritize and resolve conflicts when dependencies and initiatives multiply?
search_terms: early warning systems for org incoherence
summary: The post argues that scaling in tech predictably creates incoherence and inefficiency, but teams can reduce the damage by using enabling constraints that improve situational awareness. It’s aimed at leaders and product/engineering managers who want practical governance patterns—guardrails, gates, WIP limits, and multi-perspective review—to detect problems early during growth or uncertainty.

title: TBM 303: The Current Tech Puzzle (With Diverse Takes)
questions: Why do tech managers feel drowned by work around work and constant detail demands?
What should we sacrifice when managers are asked to get into the details but are already overloaded?
How do we reduce cognitive load and regain independent team conditions without relying on reorgs?
What interventions actually work under layoffs fear, high volatility, and constant priority shifts?
summary: The post diagnoses a recurring dysfunction in tech companies where managers are overwhelmed by administrative overhead, meetings, dependencies, and detail-chasing, amplified by layoffs fear and market volatility. It argues that fixing the problem requires removing low-value work, limiting WIP and planning inventory, and establishing a sustainable organizational tempo—along with explicit leadership behavior and incentive alignment. It’s aimed at leaders, engineering managers, and product/portfolio stakeholders trying to restore focus and independence under chaotic conditions.

title: TBM 304: Losing a Day a Week to Inefficiencies?
questions: Why don’t developers and teams revolt when they believe they lose a day a week to inefficiencies?
How do context switching and “degraded mode” normalize waste in knowledge work?
What prevents organizations from turning DevEx and productivity complaints into lasting operational excellence?
What cultural conditions are needed to enable honest feedback loops and system-level improvement?
search_terms: developer productivity waste 8 hours a week
devex fixes fail or only last briefly
summary: The post examines research suggesting developers lose roughly a day per week to inefficiencies and argues that the lack of visible “mutiny” is explained by how knowledge-work waste is distributed, normalized, and handled via workarounds. It’s aimed at engineering leaders and practitioners who want to understand why DevEx/productivity problems persist and what cultural shifts (fear/blame reduction, candid feedback loops) are required to address them systemically.

title: TBM 290: The Failure Threshold
questions: Is our organization above the failure threshold where brute-force execution is doomed?
Do manager incentives push performative rigor instead of measurable outcomes?
How can we introduce heuristic approaches like wip limits and prioritization without triggering resistance?
What architectural or business-model changes could permanently reduce cross-team friction?
summary: The post argues that product and org execution breaks down once dependencies and coordination overhead exceed a “failure threshold,” making brute-force planning mathematically unreliable. It’s aimed at product leaders and managers who need practical ways to manage interdependent work—using heuristics like wip limits, force ranking, and wsjf—while navigating incentives that reward overly rigorous plans. It also prompts readers to consider organizational, architectural, and strategy changes that reduce the need to do everything at once.

title: TBM 267: Debt and Bridge Building
questions: How should we define technical debt so it matches what the team is actually experiencing?
What practices prevent technical debt from compounding into an unmanageable mess?
When is it worth refactoring now versus adding quick fixes?
Is a fit-for-purpose gap really technical debt, or is it a migration problem?
search_terms: pragmatic quality vs quick and dirty
A/B test future scenarios technical debt
how to work down technical debt
summary: This post uses a bridge-building metaphor to explain that “technical debt” can mean very different things depending on how teams build and maintain systems over time. It is aimed at software teams and leaders who want practical ways to decide between quick fixes, refactoring as you go, and planned migrations when the system no longer fits future needs.

title: TBM 266: Human Load Balancers, Renegades, Insiders, and Platform Saviors (Part 4)
questions: How can dependency challenges get so bad before companies respond, and why does the problem creep up over time?
What early signals indicate dependencies are increasing at a dangerous rate?
How can leaders create situational awareness of the front line pain caused by dependencies?
How should organizations design incentives and platform investment so they reduce dependencies rather than just mask them?
summary: This post explains why dependency problems in fast-growing companies often go unnoticed until they severely impact cycle time and outcomes. It frames the issue through four archetypes—human load balancers, renegades, insiders, and platform saviors—and argues that each can rationally mute symptoms while delaying real fixes. It’s aimed at leaders and engineering/product managers trying to detect early signals and design incentives and platform investment that actually reduce dependencies.

title: TBM 264: Dependencies In Fast(er) Growing Companies (Part 3)
questions: What collaboration or working model should we adopt for this dependency-heavy effort?
Are we forcing teams to collaborate transactionally when they should be more embedded, or forcing collaboration when we should be more transactional?
Why are our dependencies so hard to wrangle—misalignment, incentives, interfaces, or siloed knowledge?
How do WIP and long planning horizons worsen dependency management?
summary: The post discusses how to manage dependencies in fast-growing organizations by treating collaboration as a spectrum from fully embedded to transactional. It’s aimed at leaders and delivery teams who need a practical way to decide what working model an initiative deserves, diagnose whether they’re facing misalignment (problem A) or interface friction (problem B), and reduce coordination overhead. It also highlights how WIP and long planning horizons can drive premature convergence that makes dependency wrangling harder.

title: TBM 262: Dependencies In Fast(er) Growing Companies (Part 2)
questions: When do dependencies force a shift to org-wide prioritization and capacity allocation?
What tie-breaking mechanisms work when multiple teams depend on each other?
How do WIP limits and slack change the way dependencies are surfaced and handled?
Why do common dependency-management tactics (planning inventories, high utilization, traffic-cop managers) make outcomes worse?
summary: This post examines how cross-team dependencies become harder as companies grow faster, arguing that dependency management requires broader prioritization, WIP control, and org-wide capacity thinking. It is aimed at leaders and practitioners scaling product development who want to understand why dependency tactics often fail when incentives, planning, trust, and slack are misaligned.

title: TBM 261: Dependencies In Fast(er) Growing Companies (Part 1)
questions: How do you manage ad-hoc dependencies in a high-growth environment without slowing everything down?
What prevents dependency-driven complexity from causing premature convergence and trust collapse?
How do you avoid the “just make the meeting stop” syndrome while still coordinating effectively?
Why do dependencies proliferate even when the organization has formal structure?
search_terms: managing dependencies in fast growing companies
summary: This post explores why ad-hoc, work-related dependencies are especially damaging in fast-growing companies, where speed incentives and informal culture can mask the true cost. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who need practical ways to coordinate across teams without increasing cognitive load, work-in-progress, lead times, and trust erosion. The piece frames the dependency problem as a coordination and information-flow failure that can lead to meeting-driven agreement without real alignment.

title: TBM 248: Project-ish
questions: How do limiting constraints change the probability of project success?
What makes a project 'project-ish' versus not worth calling a project?
How can teams recognize and manage Big Messy Projects early?
What organizational practices reduce coordination overhead and schedule slippage in highly interdependent work?
search_terms: starting together working together finishing together
RACI meetings about meetings project failure
summary: The post argues that work exists on a spectrum from low-constraint “team utopia” to high-constraint “unsolvable maze,” where increasing constraints drive toil, cognitive load, and risk. It introduces the idea of “project-ishness” and defines Big Messy Projects as highly interdependent, cross-team initiatives that require explicit naming and dedicated collective coordination to avoid deterioration. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners trying to improve delivery outcomes in complex, cross-functional work.

title: TBM 209: Fog and Debt Debt
questions: How do we prevent organizational or delivery “fog” before it makes diagnosis impossible?
What leading indicators should we track to catch degradation early?
Why do teams get stuck in whack-a-mole problem solving, and how can they break the cycle?
What practices help stabilize a system when accumulated stress and debt compound?
search_terms: stabilize the system lighten the load
summary: This post explains how deteriorating system health creates “fog” that impairs a team’s ability to diagnose and address problems, leading to a self-reinforcing cycle of stress and poor decisions. It argues that waiting for bad outcomes or relying on lagging indicators is dangerous, and that prevention through leading signals, situational awareness, and operational constraints is more effective than trying to “dig out” later. It’s aimed at leaders and teams managing complex delivery environments who need practical ways to reduce compounded debt and decision fatigue.

title: WIP, Multi-Tasking, Context Switching (Video)
questions: How should I explain WIP limits to people who aren’t familiar with lean theory?
What are the practical effects of multi-tasking and context switching on product delivery?
How does high WIP contribute to plans going stale and delayed value?
summary: The post argues for communicating WIP reduction using concrete, observable consequences—multi-tasking, context switching, stale plans, and delayed delivery—rather than abstract lean terminology. It’s aimed at product and delivery teams (and managers) who need to explain flow and WIP concepts in a way that resonates with non-experts.

title: TBM 40/52: Why Limiting WIP, Starting Together, Being Less Busy, and Working Together is SO HARD
questions: Why is it so difficult to implement WIP limits and starting together in engineering teams?
How can we normalize being idle or waiting without harming delivery outcomes?
What incentives and promotion criteria keep teams optimizing for busy work instead of leverage?
What trojan-horse experiments could nudge the system toward lower WIP and better collaboration?
search_terms: trojan horse experiment for process change
information flow visualize all work in progress
summary: This post explains why reducing work in progress, normalizing idle time, and coordinating “starting together” are so difficult in software/product teams, even when everyone agrees the goal is high-leverage work. It frames the problem as a complex systems and incentives issue—where promotion, specialization, staffing, and information/visibility dynamics push teams back toward busy, isolated work. It’s for leaders and practitioners trying to change engineering workflow and collaboration patterns using experiments and system leverage points rather than simple policy changes.

title: TBM 36/52: Looking at Example Boards
questions: How much work in progress is too much for a team to maintain flow and focus?
What does it mean when solution exploration is delegated versus done collaboratively?
Why do developers and designers appear unpaired, and how does that affect research and analysis involvement?
How should teams structure release stages and manage feedback monitoring after iteration?
search_terms: designer developer pairing vs individual work
summary: The post analyzes multiple real-world-inspired kanban board layouts to infer how product, design, and development work is actually being coordinated. It’s aimed at product and delivery teams that want to improve flow by visualizing non-coding work, reducing work in progress, and clarifying where collaboration (e.g., solution exploration) happens. The author invites readers to compare observations and use the boards as a diagnostic tool for team process health.

title: TBM 34/52: 12 Traps When Visualizing Work (and Managing WIP)
questions: What are the common traps teams fall into when visualizing work to manage WIP?
How can teams visualize work at multiple resolutions without losing dependencies and initiative context?
What should be shown on a kanban board to make queues, blocked work, and rework visible?
How do OKRs and the North Star Framework help connect daily work to larger outcomes?
summary: The post outlines 12 practical traps teams encounter when trying to visualize work for better WIP and flow management, such as losing the bigger picture, hiding queues/blocked work, and tracking only one resolution. It’s aimed at delivery and product teams using kanban-style boards who want more accurate modeling of value streams, collaboration, and rework. It also connects this visualization mindset to outcome alignment using OKRs and the North Star Framework.

title: TBM 23b/52: Cognitive Load
questions: How can we track and manage organizational cognitive energy over time?
Why do throughput and ticket-based visualization fail to explain knowledge work outcomes?
What happens to decision quality when teams face context switching, interruptions, and prolonged uncertainty?
How should managers protect teams without merely shifting complexity elsewhere?
summary: The post argues that managing knowledge work requires treating cognitive load and decision quality as first-class concerns, not just tracking tickets or time spent. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who want to understand how complexity, interruptions, and collaboration patterns affect organizational “energy” and recovery. It also emphasizes the need for safe, honest conversations about strain as a signal for improving how teams work together.

title: TBM 12/53: Not BAU
questions: How should teams manage work when remote work is not business as usual?
What practices reduce tension and anxiety while maintaining delivery?
How do WIP limits, smaller batches, and slack help during uncertainty?
How can teams prioritize support and community during major transitions?
search_terms: sustainable pace and wellbeing at work
summary: The post argues that pandemic remote work requires a shift away from “business as usual” productivity thinking toward sustainability, support, and reduced operational strain. It is aimed at managers and teams trying to deliver effectively from home by limiting WIP and planning inventory, working in smaller batches, and leaving slack for care and community. It also frames scope cuts as acceptable when the team prioritizes mutual support.
